front.title: Base YANG Model for any Service

abstract.t: This document describes problems with the currently published IETF service modules and defines a modular service modeling structure that extracts technology-agnostic constructs into a base ietf-svc module, separates Ethernet-specific treatment into ietf-eth-svc, and layers VPN-oriented intent in ietf-vpn-svc, with bis versions of L2VPN and L3VPN services augmenting the shared foundation.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The Layer 2 Service Model (L2SM) and Layer 3 Service Model (L3SM) YANG models provide a standardized approach to modeling L2VPN and L3VPN services. However, these models do not interact with each other, and are not designed to be extensible so that additional services can be added.

This document describes and new, extensible archicture for services modules and defines a YANG module for that can be used as the base for any customer service. The goals of this work are to reduce duplication between existing L2SM and L3SM work, enable new service types to reuse common abstractions, and let operators expose a consistent customer-facing interface while retaining flexibility for technology-specific extensions.

section.name: Limitations of the current Service Models
section.t: The following sections describe problems with the approach taken by the current L2SM and L3SM modules.

section.name: High Levels of Duplication between the Service Models
section.t: The L2SM and L3SM individually cover all the inputs to deliver either L2VPN or L3VPN services, including an abstracted view of the operator's site locations. This means that an operator that offers both types of services must manage two separate models with a large amount of duplicated information.
When additional service models are defined for new service models, the currently approach requires further duplication, making the problem worse.
section.name: Limited Interoperability and Extensibility
section.t: The L2SM and L3SM capture Layer 2 and Layer 3 VPN service intent at an abstraction level that is applicable to the agreements between an operator and their customers. This means it is difficult to express certain relationships between the L2VPN and L3VPN services being ordered, such as diversity or bandwidth constraints. This is especially important in deployment scenarios where common infrastructure hardware is used for providing both L2VPN and L3VPN services.

**The next part is describing a solution and would probably be better later on** More broadly, operators and their customers would benefit from a common service modeling framework that can be reused across different service types, and that can capture the relationships and constraints.
Examples of services that could be modeled on top of the same base include the already standardized L2VPN and L3VPN services, but also non-VPN services such as (business) internet access, cloud connectivity, or even optical wavelength services. The common service model would allow operators to maintain a consistent customer-facing interface across different service types, and to more easily introduce new service types in the future.

section.name: Missing Support for Network Technology Layering
section.t: The current L2SM and L3SM models do not have a way to express the relationships between the different technology layers that are used to provision the final service. For example, an operator may want to specify that an L3VPN service should be delivered over a specific underlying L2VPN service, or that an SD-WAN service should be delivered over a specific Internet access service. The lack of support for network layering makes it difficult to model and manage complex service offerings that involve multiple layers of abstraction and different technologies.

section.name: Complexity in Creating a Single View of Services
section.t: Currently, the L2SM and L3SM do not contain operational state. If these modules are extended with state nodes, e.g., so it is possible to retrieve information about the current health of customer's services, the separation of L2SM, L3SM, and other service models makes correlating the service impact of underlying failures affecting more than one service type more difficult.

section.name: Over-abstraction Hinders Clear Agreements
section.t:
**Is this a problem with the current model architecture or a shortcoming in the L3SM specifics?**
The L3SM abstracts away from the underlying network technologies and focuses on the service intent. While this is a strength in terms of technology-agnostic modeling, it can also make it difficult to capture certain service attributes that are relevant to the customer or to the delivery of the service.
For example, the L3SM has no method of specifying a port and/or VLAN on a Site Device on which a Site Network Access should be delivered. This can lead to ambiguity in the service specification, and it can make it difficult for operators to ensure that the service is delivered according to the customer's intent.
The constraints mechanism goes through great lengths to avoid identifying the specific (number of) bearers that should be used to deliver a service. The ordering process for many operators today is much more explicit in naming the bearers, along with an explicit mapping of each Site Network Access to those bearers. At least giving the purchaser the option of providing their requirements in such an explicit manner would guarantee that the operator can deliver a service according to the customer's actual requirements.

section.name: Proposed Solution
section.t: This document revisits the existing L2SM and L2SM models, introducing a new structure with a common base model. This removes the duplication between service models, and enables much simpler interworking between different service types. It also forms an extensbile basis for the definition of new service models in the future.

The core of the module structure is defined in ietf-svc. This provides a technology-agnostic service skeleton covering sites, site-network-accesses, diversity constraints, and bearer hints. Additionally, Ethernet-centric constructs such as bandwidth profiles and QoS classification are moved into a dedicated ietf-eth-svc module so that Ethernet-based services can share the same primitives without cluttering the core service skeleton.
The ietf-vpn-svc module builds on this define VPN-oriented attributes such as service type, topology, cloud, and extranet reachability, carriers' carrier, and policy filters.

Finally, the technology specific paramters for for L2VPN and L3VPN services are augmented into the shared service framework.The L3VPN module retains the routing, multicast, address-allocation, NAT, and BFD constructs from . The L2VPN module retains the Ethernet- and OAM-specific behaviors from (e.g., bundling, CE-VLAN preservation, L2CP handling, CFM, and Y.1731) while leveraging the shared service skeleton, the Ethernet treatment constructs in ietf-eth-svc, and the VPN service identities from ietf-vpn-svc.

The augment relationships across the modules are shown in ; the path is linear, with ietf-svc at the root, ietf-eth-svc augmenting the base skeleton, and ietf-vpn-svc augmenting both ietf-eth-svc and ietf-svc before the VPN-specific bis modules attach.

figure.name: Service module augmentation relationships
figure.artwork: module: ietf-svc (Base service skeleton) +-- augment: ietf-eth-svc (Ethernet attributes, e.g QoS) +-- augment: ietf-vpn-svc (VPN service common attributes) +-- augment: ietf-l2vpn-svc (L2VPN-specific attributes) +-- augment: ietf-l3vpn-svc (L3VPN-specific attributes)

section.t: By aligning the L2SM and L3SM models on a common foundation, this work reduces duplication, improves consistency of service exposure to customers and OSS/BSS systems, and simplifies the introduction of future service types that can reuse the same base abstractions.

section.name: Relationships to other work
section.t: The network layer models, iet-l3vpn-ntw and iet-l2vpn-ntw may benefit from a similar modularization approach, but they are outside the scope of this document.
Adoption of the common groupings defined in ietf-vpn-common at the service model level may be beneficial and should be considered as part of this work.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: The security posture of this document follows the guidance in the L3SM . The NETCONF Access Control Model (NACM) should be used to restrict protocol operations and data nodes to authorized principals.

New augments added to the base service skeleton must describe any additional security-sensitive leaves and specify suitable NACM rules. When encryption or authentication material is conveyed (e.g., keys or credentials), operators must ensure secure transport and operational processes (rotation, revocation, audit) consistent with their policy.
